title: Anger at the Country That Won't Protect Its Children
dek: When school shootings become routine and safety nets unravel, the anger you feel isn't a problem to solve. It's information your body holds about values violated and children endangered. This rage, properly understood and directed, becomes the engine of protection our institutions refuse to provide.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Rage at systems that fail children is not irrational emotion but moral clarity demanding expression. Suppressing this anger protects institutional comfort while abandoning the vulnerable people we claim to cherish. Learning to channel collective fury into sustained action transforms grief into protection.

You've been told that your anger at a country that won't protect its children is unproductive. That's the first lie. The second is that your fury makes you part of the problem. The third is that if you just calm down and speak reasonably, the people with power will finally listen. None of these are true. Your anger is not the obstacle to change. It's the fuel. I'm writing this in a week when another school became a crime scene, when another set of parents received the call that ends a life even if their body keeps breathing. In my own practice, I've sat with mothers whose hands shake when they drop their children at school, with teachers who've mapped escape routes in their sleep, with teenagers who've practiced bleeding control because adults handed them tourniquets instead of safety. The anger in that room isn't pathology. It's sanity responding to insanity. ## The Body Knows What the Mind Tries to Excuse Your nervous system registers threat faster than your conscious mind can rationalize it away. When you see another news alert, another press conference with the same script, another round of thoughts and prayers, your shoulders tighten. Your jaw sets. Your breathing shallows. This is not anxiety disorder. This is your body correctly identifying that the people tasked with protection have abandoned the post. Psychologist Harriet Lerner writes extensively about how anger serves as a signal that something important is at stake, that a boundary has been violated or a value compromised. When children die in buildings designed for learning, every protective instinct you possess fires at once. The anger isn't disproportionate. The response from institutions is. In our experience working with people who carry rage about systemic failures, the body often holds the anger long after the mind has tried to file it away. You might notice tension that doesn't release with stretching, a startle response that's grown hair-trigger, digestive issues that flare when the news cycle spins up again. These aren't separate from your anger at a country that won't protect its children. They're how that anger lives in tissue when you have no safe place to put it. The CDC's guidance on stress and the body documents how chronic activation of threat response systems affects everything from immune function to cardiovascular health. What they don't say explicitly is that living in a country that treats child death as an acceptable cost of political gridlock keeps those systems activated without resolution. ## When Collective Rage Has Nowhere to Go There's a particular quality to anger that has no outlet. It curdles. It turns inward. It becomes depression, anxiety, a grinding sense that nothing you do matters because the machinery is too big and too broken. You start to feel crazy for caring this much when everyone else seems to have moved on by the next news cycle. Buddhist teacher Thich Nhat Hanh taught that anger, when acknowledged and held with awareness, can be transformed into compassion and understanding. But he also recognized that this transformation requires space and support, not immediate suppression. The anger at systems that fail children needs to be felt fully before it can be worked with skillfully. Bypassing straight to forgiveness or acceptance without honoring the rage is spiritual gaslighting. Over the years I've seen people tie themselves in knots trying to be the reasonable voice, the one who doesn't let emotion cloud judgment, the one who can see both sides. Meanwhile, children practice active shooter drills that traumatize them in the name of preparation for violence that doesn't have to exist. Your anger at this absurdity is proportional. The cultural insistence that you moderate it is not. Some people channel this rage into organizing. They show up at school board meetings, at legislative sessions, at protests that the news cameras ignore unless something dramatic happens. Some write letters that go unanswered. Some donate to organizations doing the work they can't do themselves. Some research candidates and vote and phone bank. And some do all of this and still feel the anger building because the pace of change is glacial and children are dying now, today, while we wait for incremental progress. ## The Anger Practice You Weren't Taught What if anger at a country that won't protect its children doesn't need to be fixed? What if it needs to be honored, directed, and sustained? Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to the idea that anger is information, not pathology. It tells you what you value. It shows you where boundaries have been violated. It mobilizes energy for protection and change. The practice isn't to eliminate the anger. It's to keep it from consuming you while you figure out what to do with it. This is not the same as calming down. It's not anger management in the corporate sense of making yourself palatable to systems that prefer your silence. It's learning to carry heat without burning out. Therapist Beverly Engel has written about how anger, particularly righteous anger at injustice, can be a powerful force for personal and social change when it's acknowledged rather than suppressed. The key is distinguishing between anger that clarifies and anger that clouds, between rage that mobilizes and rage that paralyzes. Both start from the same fire. The difference is what you do next. For some people, herbal support helps create enough nervous system space to feel the anger without being swallowed by it. [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support the body's stress response when you're running on fumes and fury. [Solgar GABA 500 mg (50 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z8WXMU?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps some people find a steadier baseline when the activation feels constant. These aren't solutions. They're supports while you do the harder work of figuring out how to channel rage into sustained action. ## A Five-Minute Practice for When the Anger Feels Impossible You can do this standing or sitting, anywhere you have a few minutes alone. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Breathe normally. Don't try to change anything yet. Just notice where the anger lives in your body right now. Is it in your throat, tight and hot? In your shoulders, wound up like springs? In your gut, churning and acidic? Name the location silently. "The anger is in my jaw. The anger is in my fists." Now speak directly to it, out loud if you can, silently if you must: "I feel you. You're here because I care about children being safe. You're here because the people with power to change this have chosen not to. You're allowed to be here." Take three deliberate breaths, expanding your belly on the inhale, letting everything go on the exhale. On the fourth breath, ask the anger what it needs from you. Not what it needs from politicians or systems or other people. What it needs from you, today, in the next hour or week. Sometimes the answer is rest. Sometimes it's action. Sometimes it's just acknowledgment that you see it and you're not going to pretend it away. This practice doesn't resolve anything. It gives you a way to check in with the anger instead of white-knuckling through or collapsing under it. You can return to it as many times as you need. ## The Isolation of Caring This Much One of the cruelest aspects of rage at systemic failure is how isolating it becomes. You look around at people going about their lives, posting vacation photos, complaining about traffic, and you think: how are you not on fire? How are you functional when children died yesterday and will die again next week and the month after that? Some of those people are numb. Some are in denial. Some have decided that if they let themselves feel the full weight of it they'll never get out of bed again. Some are doing the same thing you're doing, holding it together in public and falling apart in private. You can't always tell from the outside who's carrying what. The isolation gets worse when you try to talk about it and people change the subject, or tell you that getting this worked up isn't healthy, or suggest that if you really cared you'd focus on solutions instead of problems. As if anger and action were mutually exclusive. As if the people doing the most to protect children weren't often the ones burning with the most fury at systems that make that protection necessary. You need people who can hold this with you. Not people who'll talk you down or help you see the bright side. People who'll say yes, this is enraging, yes, children are dying, yes, the people with power to stop it have chosen their comfort over kids' lives. People who won't flinch from the rage or try to fix it. Finding even one person who can be present with your anger without trying to manage it is worth more than a dozen who mean well but can't sit with the heat. ## What Happens When You Stop Trying to Be Reasonable There's a script for how you're supposed to engage with injustice. Be informed but not strident. Be passionate but not emotional. Care deeply but don't let it show too much because that makes people uncomfortable and uncomfortable people stop listening. Present facts calmly. Acknowledge complexity. Respect different perspectives. Build bridges. Find common ground. All of that has its place. But when children are dying and the common ground is a graveyard, reasonableness becomes collaboration with violence. Not active collaboration. Passive. The kind where you participate in the fiction that this is a both-sides issue requiring nuanced debate instead of a moral emergency requiring immediate action. What happens when you stop performing reasonableness? Sometimes you lose people. Friends who can't handle the intensity. Family members who think you've become too political. Colleagues who wish you'd just keep it professional. This loss is real and it hurts. But what you gain is integrity. You gain the ability to look at yourself and know you didn't trade children's safety for social comfort. You also gain clarity. When you stop moderating your anger to make it acceptable, you see more clearly who's willing to stand in the fire with you and who needs you to pretend the fire isn't there. You see which organizations are doing real work and which are performing concern. You see where your energy actually makes a difference and where you've been spinning wheels in systems designed to absorb and neutralize exactly this kind of rage. For nervous system support during this process, some people find [Real Mushrooms Lion's Mane Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B084JVN2VT?tag=spankyspinola-20) helpful for mental clarity when everything feels overwhelming. You can also explore [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) for other plant allies that support you in staying present with difficult emotion without dissociating or burning out. ## The Long Game of Righteous Fury Anger at injustice can't be a sprint. It has to become something you can sustain over years, maybe decades, maybe a lifetime. This isn't about keeping yourself constantly activated at peak rage. That'll destroy you. It's about finding a way to stay connected to the truth of what's happening without that truth annihilating your capacity to function. Some days you'll wake up and the anger will be the first thing you feel, heavy on your chest before you even open your eyes. Other days it'll recede to background hum, present but not consuming. Both are fine. The anger doesn't have to be constant to be real. You don't have to perform it to prove you care. What matters is that you don't let the culture of comfort convince you that your rage is the problem. What matters is that you find ways to channel it that don't require you to set yourself on fire. What matters is that you remember: the anger at a country that won't protect its children is not pathology. It's love in its most fierce and uncompromising form. The work of sustaining righteous anger over time is both personal and collective. Personally, you need practices that help you process and release what you can't carry, whether that's therapy, movement, art, time in nature, or the support of [more articles in this journal](/articles) that help you understand what you're experiencing. Collectively, you need community with others who refuse to normalize the unacceptable, who'll remind you on hard days that your anger is justified and necessary. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I stay angry without becoming bitter or cynical? Bitterness is what happens when anger has nowhere to go and no one witnesses it. The antidote isn't to stop being angry. It's to find containers for the anger that honor it without letting it poison everything else. This might mean regular venting sessions with trusted people, physical practices that move the energy through your body, or directing the anger into concrete action however small. Cynicism grows when you believe nothing can change. Righteous anger insists that change is both necessary and possible. ### What if my anger at this issue is affecting my relationships? It probably is, and that's not necessarily a problem. Some relationships can't survive one person waking up to injustice the other person prefers to ignore. Some relationships get stronger when both people can be honest about the rage they're carrying. The question isn't whether your anger affects your relationships but whether the people in your life can meet you in truth or only in pretense. You might need to find additional community among people doing similar work, people for whom this anger is a shared language rather than an uncomfortable topic. ### Is it okay to sometimes just not think about it so I can function? Yes. Absolutely yes. Sustained engagement with systemic horror requires breaks. You can't stay activated all the time without serious harm to your nervous system. Taking breaks isn't the same as not caring. It's not bypassing or spiritual weakness. It's basic sustainability. You're allowed to watch something stupid, to focus on your own small life, to have days where you don't engage with the news. The anger will be there when you come back. It doesn't need constant tending to stay alive. ## Where the Anger Lives Next The country that won't protect its children is the same country that will eventually change, because enough people refused to accept the unacceptable. That change won't come from reasoned debate alone. It'll come from sustained pressure fueled by anger that wouldn't die, wouldn't be managed away, wouldn't settle for incremental progress while children bled out in classrooms. Your anger is part of that pressure. Not the whole thing, but part. What you do with it matters. Whether you organize or donate or vote or write or show up or any combination of these and more, the through-line is this: you didn't let them convince you that caring this much was the problem. You didn't moderate yourself into complicity. You found a way to hold the heat and direct it outward. The work is long and the victories are small and sometimes it feels like nothing changes fast enough to matter. But systems change because people refuse to stop pushing. They change because anger, properly directed and sustained over time, is more powerful than the inertia of the status quo. Your rage at a country that won't protect its children is a gift, even when it feels like a burden. It means you're alive to what matters. It means you haven't gone numb. It means you're still in the fight, and the fight needs you exactly as you are, fury and all. For more on working with difficult emotions and finding sustainable practices, you might explore [the about page](/about) or visit [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) for additional perspectives on anger as a spiritual practice. --- *Last updated: January 2025* *Written by The Oracle Lover for [The Anger Practice](https://theangerpractice.com)* ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on anger at the country that won't protect its children, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
